content:
===BEGIN===
The axial tilt of Earth, approximately 23.5°, relative to its orbital plane is the principal cause of seasonal variation, not the Earth-Sun distance. This obliquity induces differential solar insolation across latitudes over the annual orbit. During a hemisphere’s summer solstice, its pole tilts toward the Sun, increasing solar angle and day length, thus intensifying insolation and surface heating. Conversely, during winter solstice, the pole tilts away, reducing solar elevation and daylight duration, leading to cooler conditions. The elliptical orbit produces minor insolation variation (~7%) insufficient to drive seasons. Additionally, the precession of the equinoxes modulates the timing of perihelion relative to solstices over ~23,000 years, subtly influencing seasonal intensity. This axial tilt-driven mechanism orchestrates complex climatological responses, including atmospheric and oceanic circulation shifts, underpinning the Earth’s distinct seasonal patterns beyond simplistic distance-based explanations.

===END===
